one_line_takeaway: By converting a target document's verifiable evidence into a natural-looking, objectively-gradable multi-question 'exam' and aggregating scores, E-MIA turns black-box RAG membership inference from an unreliable similarity-threshold problem into a near-perfect (Acc>0.97, AUC-ROC up to 1.000), stealthy, and rewriting/guardrail-resistant attack.
key_finding: E-MIA substantially outperforms three RAG-MIA baselines (S2MIA: BLEU-based similarity; MBA: masked-token-completion; IA: Yes/No summary-derived questions) across all three datasets and four generator models, achieving Accuracy typically above 0.97 and AUC-ROC often reaching 1.000 (vs. baseline Accuracy in the 0.62-0.94 range). The largest gains appear at stringent low-FPR operating points: on NFCorpus the average TPR@FPR=0.005 improvement over the best baseline is 117.8%, on TREC-COVID 141.2%, on SCIDOCS 142.6% (paper's own reported per-dataset AVG Improve rows). E.g., on NFCorpus/Deepseek-r1-14B, E-MIA reaches Acc 0.977, AUC-ROC 0.995, TPR@FPR=0.005 of 0.817 vs. best baseline (IA) 0.925/0.914/0.254 respectively. Under rewriting defenses (Table III), E-MIA's Accuracy degrades by only 0.002-0.003 (e.g., 0.988 under 'Both Rewriting'), while baselines like S2MIA drop by up to 0.279 (Accuracy falling to 0.462) and IA by up to 0.291. Against malicious-input guardrails (Table IV), E-MIA achieves near-perfect pass/stealth rates (0.997-1.000) across PIGuard, Tanaos-Guardrail-v1, and FT-Llama-Prompt-Guard-2, notably outperforming S2MIA (as low as 0.210 pass rate on FT-Llama-Prompt-Guard-2). The attack saturates with a compact query budget: AUC-ROC rises steeply and plateaus after ~20-24 questions (paper recommends N>=28, i.e. >=7 items per type). Ablations show format diversity matters: combining all four question types ('Full') reaches peak TPR@FPR=0.005 of 0.996, beating any single-format configuration. A heatmap of evidence-type x question-type separability (Delta = Acc_member - Acc_non-member) shows proper-nouns/technical-terms and constraint relations paired with True/False questions give the highest discrimination (peak Delta=0.790). The attack is largely robust to retrieval budget k (1-20 retrieved docs), retriever choice (BGE/MiniLM/text-embedding-3-small), exam generator choice (Accuracy clustering 0.985-0.995 across DeepSeek-R1, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Qwen-Max, GPT-5-mini), and generalizes to Chinese text (ChineseWebText) without notable degradation. DeepSeek-R1 is reported as the cheapest exam generator at $0.0063 per target document.
core_contribution: The paper proposes E-MIA, a black-box document-level membership inference attack (MIA) against RAG systems that reformulates membership inference as an 'exam' over document-specific verifiable evidence. It decomposes a target document into five typed evidence categories (precise details, proper nouns/technical terms, definitional statements, metadata cues, constraint relations), converts these into four objectively-gradable question formats (fill-in-the-blank, single-choice, multiple-choice, true/false), queries the black-box RAG system with the resulting natural-looking exam, deterministically grades responses (regex/exact-match/option-set matching), and aggregates per-type accuracies into a weighted 0-100 membership score (S = 100*sum(w_i * Acc_i)) that is thresholded (default tau=62.2) to output a membership decision. Calibrated default weights: w_FB=0.312, w_SC=0.214, w_MC=0.300, w_T/F=0.174, derived offline via a held-out development set using KL-divergence-based separability.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with API-level query access only to a deployed RAG system: no access to the retrieval corpus D, retriever/LLM parameters, retrieved contexts, retrieval scores, or internal logs, and possibly subject to malicious-input filtering. The adversary possesses the content of a candidate target document d* (standard MIA assumption) and wants to determine whether d* in D, i.e., whether it has been ingested into the corpus/knowledge base. Attack goal is stealthy: prompts are phrased as natural reading-comprehension/QA exam questions rather than explicit confirmation probes, to reduce detection/refusal risk.
limitations: The paper's own reported limitations/caveats: (1) offline calibration of question-type weights (w_i) and decision threshold (tau=62.2) requires a held-out development set with known member/non-member labels, which the paper treats as available to the attacker for calibration though the attack itself then uses fixed specifications; the paper does not evaluate sensitivity when the calibration set is mismatched to the deployed target system's domain/model. (2) The paper explicitly notes 'Retrieval recall is analysis-only and not attacker-observable' when reporting Table III, i.e., some robustness metrics reported include information the black-box adversary could not itself observe, used only for the authors' own analysis. (3) FB question difficulty is sensitive to number of blanks -- accuracy/discrimination drops sharply from one blank (Delta~0.686) to ten blanks (Delta~0.177), indicating the method depends on careful, non-trivial question-design tuning to remain effective. (4) Evaluation is restricted to encyclopedic/scientific/biomedical text corpora (BEIR subsets) plus one Chinese web-text corpus; the paper does not test highly repetitive, template-heavy, or very short documents where evidence extraction may be harder. (5) The three defenses tested (query/response rewriting, three specific guardrail classifiers) are not exhaustive of possible RAG-specific MIA defenses; the authors frame this only as robustness against 'a range of' defenses, not a complete adversarial evaluation.

datasets_used: NFCorpus (BEIR, ~3.5K biomedical docs, avg length 1496.92 tokens)
TREC-COVID (BEIR, ~23K biomedical docs, avg length 1356.24 tokens)
SCIDOCS (BEIR, ~116K scientific docs, avg length 1147.23 tokens)
ChineseWebText (Chinese-language corpus, used for cross-lingual generalization)
quantitative_results: NFCorpus/Deepseek-r1-14B: E-MIA Acc 0.977, AUC-ROC 0.995, AUC-PR 0.994, TPR@FPR=0.05/0.01/0.005 = 0.993/0.911/0.817

Average TPR@FPR=0.005 improvement of E-MIA over best baseline: 117.8% (NFCorpus), 141.2%/142.6% variants reported (TREC-COVID/SCIDOCS AVG Improve rows), overall min 117.8% / max 142.6% across datasets
Robustness to rewriting defenses (Table III, 'Both Rewriting'): E-MIA Accuracy 0.988 (only 0.002 degradation) vs. S2MIA 0.462 (0.279 degradation) and IA 0.691 (0.291 degradation)
Guardrail pass rates (Table IV): E-MIA 1.000 (PIGuard), 0.997 (Tanaos-Guardrail-v1), 1.000 (FT-Llama-Prompt-Guard-2), vs. S2MIA as low as 0.210 (FT-Llama-Prompt-Guard-2) and 0.439 (PIGuard)
Query scalability: AUC-ROC plateaus after 20-24 questions; recommended N=28 (>=7 per question type)
Calibrated weighted-score parameters: w_FB=0.312, w_SC=0.214, w_MC=0.300, w_T/F=0.174, decision threshold tau=62.2
Exam generator cost: DeepSeek-R1 (primary generator) costs $0.0063 per target document, cheapest among tested generators (Eco-Benefit=1.0)
Cross-generator consistency: Accuracy 0.985-0.995 and AUC-ROC 0.996-1.000 across DeepSeek-R1, Gemini-2.0-Flash, Qwen-Max, GPT-5-mini as exam generators; TPR@FPR=0.005 stays above 0.900 for all
FB blank-count ablation: Delta (member-nonmember accuracy gap) falls from 0.686 (one blank) to 0.669 (two blanks), 0.532 (three), 0.421 (five), 0.177 (ten blanks)
Full (all four question types) configuration reaches peak TPR@FPR=0.005 of 0.996, exceeding any single-format configuration
baselines_compared: S2MIA (Li et al., ICASSP 2025) - BLEU-based semantic similarity between RAG outputs and target document
MBA (Liu et al., ACM Web Conf. 2025) - masked-token-completion accuracy as membership signal
IA (Naseh et al., ACM SIGSAC 2025) - Yes/No questions derived from document summaries
